coredata.dc:title: Flexible self-powered ultraviolet photodetector based on the GQDs: Zinc oxide nanoflower heterojunction

coredata.dc:description: In this study, we present a flexible UV photodetector with a PET/ITO/GQDs: ZnO/PEDOS/ITO/PET structure, utilizing ZnO nanoflowers (ZnO NFs) which are more suitable for flexible devices. The close contact between the petal-like ZnO flakes helps maintain the integrity of the ZnO layer and mitigate cracking issues, while Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) fill the gaps between ZnO NFs, providing structural support and improving bending stability. Additionally, Se atoms in the PEDOS structure and oxygen-rich functional groups at the edges of GQDs interact with oxygen vacancies on the ZnO NFs surface, enhancing electron transport and further improving device performance. This flexible photodetector shows a high responsivity of up to 2.23 mA/W under room temperature and 0 V external bias (365 nm), with a detectivity of 1.949 × 1010 Jones, and response times of 0.074 s and 0.082 s. The device demonstrates good light response under different bending angles and after multiple bending cycles, maintaining its detection capability even after 1000 bends, with a responsivity of 1.02 mA/W and a detectivity of 9.930 × 109 Jones. This indicates that the device possesses excellent flexibility and stability.

head.ce:title: Flexible self-powered ultraviolet photodetector based on the GQDs: Zinc oxide nanoflower heterojunction

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Chemistry and Utilization of Carbon Based Energy Resources, College of Chemistry, Xinjiang University, Urumqi, Xinjiang 830017, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Chemistry and Utilization of Carbon Based Energy Resources, College of Chemistry, Xinjiang University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Chemistry and Utilization of Carbon Based Energy Resources; College of Chemistry, Xinjiang University, Urumqi, 830017, Xinjiang, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, we present a flexible UV photodetector with a PET/ITO/GQDs: ZnO/PEDOS/ITO/PET structure, utilizing ZnO nanoflowers (ZnO NFs) which are more suitable for flexible devices. The close contact between the petal-like ZnO flakes helps maintain the integrity of the ZnO layer and mitigate cracking issues, while Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) fill the gaps between ZnO NFs, providing structural support and improving bending stability. Additionally, Se atoms in the PEDOS structure and oxygen-rich functional groups at the edges of GQDs interact with oxygen vacancies on the ZnO NFs surface, enhancing electron transport and further improving device performance. This flexible photodetector shows a high responsivity of up to 2.23 mA/W under room temperature and 0 V external bias (365 nm), with a detectivity of 1.949 × 1010 Jones, and response times of 0.074 s and 0.082 s. The device demonstrates good light response under different bending angles and after multiple bending cycles, maintaining its detection capability even after 1000 bends, with a responsivity of 1.02 mA/W and a detectivity of 9.930 × 109 Jones. This indicates that the device possesses excellent flexibility and stability.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: UV detectors are devices that convert incident UV radiation into electrical signals and find applications in fields such as missile guidance, ultraviolet communication, skin detection, flame detection, and pollution monitoring [1,2]. To perform effectively in demanding environments, these detectors require increasingly advanced functionalities. However, traditional UV detectors, which are often made on rigid substrates like monocrystalline silicon, quartz, and sapphire, are susceptible to brittleness and breakage under bending or mechanical stress. This fragility limits their usability in challenging conditions [3,4].

Flexible UV detectors offer excellent photoelectric properties and present unique advantages, including being lightweight, impact-resistant, and capable of withstanding significant deformations such as stretching, compression, and bending [5]. These characteristics make them highly suitable for flexible wearable sensors with considerable development potential [6]. Currently, flexible substrates used in UV detectors include materials such as polyimide (PI) [7], polyethylene naphthalate (PEN) [8,9], polyethylene terephthalate (PET) [10,11], polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) [12], polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) [13], polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE), carbon cloth [14], polyester fiber [15], mica [16], and paper substrates [17]. For example, Trung et al. [18] demonstrated a UV photodetector based on a 3D micropatterned stretchable substrate with PbS quantum dots (QDs) and ZnO nanorods (NRs) as the optical sensing layer, which is responsive to UV, visible, and near-infrared light.

ZnO, GaN, TiO₂, SnO₂, and similar materials are commonly used in UV photodetectors due to their broad responsiveness to specific ultraviolet light wavelengths. Among these, ZnO is notable as a typical broadband semiconductor with a bandgap of 3.37 eV, which corresponds to the ultraviolet region of the spectrum [17,19–23]. ZnO offers excellent electrical properties, low toxicity, high UV absorption coefficient, good stability, strong visible light transmittance, and effective absorption in the ultraviolet range. In addition, ZnO has pyroelectric effect and piezoelectric photoelectric effect, which will cause the device to exhibit better performance under bending conditions. ZnO has a high piezoelectric constant compared to piezoelectric semiconductors such as GaN, ZnS and InN [24–26]. This makes ZnO an ideal choice for flexible UV PDs [27,28]. However, ZnO materials can have inherent surface imperfections that lead to longer response times, low dark currents, and reduced responsivity. For instance, Yang et al. [17] developed a UV photodetector using ZnO and polyethylene glycol (PEG) composites on A4 paper, which achieved a response rate of 177 mA/W. Despite this, the extended rise time of 15 seconds limits its practical applications.

To address the challenges mentioned, strategies such as doping modification, surface treatment, and the construction of p-n heterojunctions are commonly employed. Initially, an organic-inorganic p-n heterojunction is created to establish an inherent electric field. This field facilitates the rapid separation of electron-hole pairs generated by photoexcitation, directing them towards opposite ends and thereby enhancing both photocurrent and response time. Common p-type organic semiconductor materials used in these applications include polypyrrole (PPy) [29], poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene) (PEDOT) [30,31], polyaniline (PANI) [32], and poly(3,4-ethylenedioxyselenium) (PEDOS) [33], Shahruz et al. [34] successfully fabricated a PANI/ZnO heterojunction UV photodetector, which operates at zero bias and demonstrates reproducible and remarkable photoresponsivity. The device exhibits high photocurrent, long-term stability, substantial UV on/off switching capability, and low rise and fall times. PEDOS and its derivatives offer several advantages due to their low redox potential, electrochromism, charge transport properties, and narrow bandgap. With an optical bandgap of 1.4 eV, PEDOS can accommodate more charge when doped compared to thiophene analogs, due to its lower redox potential. Additionally, the polarizability of selenium atoms enhances charge transport through intermolecular selenium-selenium interactions [35–37]. By forming a p-n heterojunction with ZnO, PEDOS can effectively reduce surface defects and oxygen vacancies in ZnO, resulting in a flexible UV detector with improved responsiveness and shortened response times while maintaining self-powered detection capabilities [38].

In addition, surface modification was employed to enhance the performance of ZnO NFs. Quantum dots are quasi-zero-dimensional nanostructures, typically spherical, with all three dimensions measured in nanometers. This structure provides them with unique advantages in optoelectronics. Among these, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are composed of one or more layers of graphene sheets with lateral dimensions of less than 10 nm. GQDs exhibit excellent electronic, thermal, and mechanical properties and are widely used in applications such as solar cells, supercapacitors, and biosensors [39,40] The GQDs are dispersed as photosensitizers inside the ZnO NFs sheet layer, and co-generate photogenerated carriers with ZnO NFs during UV irradiation to enhance the photocurrent of the device. Meanwhile, after the device is bent, the introduction of GQDs can reduce the generation of cracks in the ZnO layer to a certain extent, or bridge the electron transport in the cracks. [41] Yin et al. [42] have presented a flexible UV photodetector based on GQDs@ZnO NWs. The integration of GQDs improves the device sensitivity by a factor of three and shortens the response time by a factor of four.

In this study, we designed a flexible self-powered UV PD featuring a ZnO NFs structure modified with GQDs, combined with PEDOS to form a p-n heterojunction. The Se atoms in the PEDOS structure and the oxygen-rich functional groups at the edges of the GQDs interact with the oxygen vacancies on the ZnO NFs surface, resulting in excellent performance of the device in its unbent state. Under room temperature and 0 V external bias (365 nm), the device achieves a maximum responsivity of 1.86 mA/W, a maximum detectivity of 1.96 × 1010 Jones, and optimal rise/fall times of 0.082/0.084 s. The close contact between the ZnO plate-like "petals" helps maintain the integrity of the ZnO layer and alleviate cracking issues, while GQDs fill the gaps between the ZnO NFs layers, providing higher bending stability during bending. The flexible photodetector shows a response speed of up to 2.23 mA/W, a detectivity of 1.949 × 1010 Jones, and response times of 0.074 s and 0.082 s under 60° bending conditions. The device maintains excellent light response even after 1000 bending cycles, with a responsivity of 1.02 mA/W and a detectivity of 9.930 × 109 Jones, demonstrating its outstanding flexibility and stability.

ZnO was deposited onto a flexible ITO/PET substrate using the I-T mode technique. A 0.1 M zinc nitrate solution in distilled water was used as the electrolyte. During the electrochemical polymerization process, ITO/PET, platinum, and Ag/AgCl served as the working, counter, and reference electrodes, respectively, within a three-electrode electrochemical cell controlled by electrochemical instrumentation (CHI760E). The I-T mode parameters were set to a voltage of −0.75 V for a duration of 2700 seconds. After deposition, the ITO/PET substrate coated with ZnO was rinsed with distilled water and air-dried [43]. Details of the preparation and polymerization process of EDOS are provided in the supporting information (SI) and Fig. S1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of GQDs and GQDs: ZnO
ce:section.ce:para: In the described procedure, 0.03 g of pitch carbon fibers were added to a solution of 2 mL HNO3 and 6 mL concentrated H2SO4. The mixture was then agitated and sonicated at 80°C for two hours. After cooling, the solution was diluted with 80 mL of deionized water, and the pH was adjusted to 8 by adding Na2CO3. The resulting solution, with a molecular weight of 1000 Da, was dialyzed in a dialysis bag for three days [34]. UV excitation photos of the prepared GQDs are shown in Fig. S2.
Following the production of GQDs, the solution containing the GQDs was spin-coated onto the ZnO/ITO/PET surface and then dried in an oven at 60°C for two hours.

The device preparation process is illustrated in Fig. 1(a). Two flexible ITO/PET substrates are used for the growth of ZnO nanofibers (NFs) and PEDOS. After the film is formed, GQDs are spin-coated onto the ZnO NFs layer. The two substrates are then laminated together with their faces in contact, and a PVA layer is wrapped around them to create the test device. A p-n heterojunction is established between the ZnO (n-type) and PEDOS (p-type) layers. The integrated electric field formed by the ZnO-PEDOS junction swiftly releases photogenerated carriers, reducing the impact of defects on the ZnO NFs surface and improving device operation. The incorporation of GQDs increases the number of photogenerated carriers, enhancing the device’s performance. Additionally, GQDs support the ZnO NFs layer, helping to maintain the structural integrity of the ZnO film during bending and reducing the occurrence of cracks. The PVA wrapping enhances the cohesion between the two layers and minimizes carrier spillover, ensuring high efficiency of the device under UV light irradiation.

Analyzing the morphological characteristics of electrochemically grown ZnO nanofibers (NFs) and the incorporation of GQDs, as shown in the subsequent figures, provides additional insight. In Fig. 1(b), the SEM image of ZnO NFs reveals a uniform structure with petal-like formations in close contact, some of which are stacked. This configuration creates an efficient pathway for electron transport. Furthermore, the petal-like three-dimensional structure increases the surface area exposed to light, enhancing UV light absorption and, consequently, the photocurrent of the UV detector. Fig. 1(c) presents a high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) image of ZnO, showing lattice stripe spacing of 0.27 nm, consistent with literature reports [39]. Fig. S3 displays a TEM image of ZnO NFs that illustrates their structural arrangement, which facilitates the transfer of photogenerated carriers between layers. This design allows UV light that is not absorbed by the ZnO monolayer to pass through to subsequent layers, thereby improving the sensitivity of the UV detector. Additionally, Fig. 1(d) shows the HR-TEM image of GQDs within ZnO NFs. The GQDs, with particle sizes around 5 nm, are embedded within the ZnO NFs layer, ensuring their effective role as photosensitizers in the device. Fig. 1(e) presents the SEM image of electrochemically polymerized PEDOS, revealing a flat layer with a uniform texture composed of small particles. This surface topology enhances the contact between PEDOS and the ZnO NFs, improving device performance. Elemental mapping in Fig. 1(f) and EDS analysis in Fig. 1(g) show a uniform distribution of elements, with carbon content at 0.9 %, confirming the consistent loading of GQDs onto the ZnO substrate. The UV–visible absorption spectra in Fig. 1(h) show absorption peaks around 350 nm for both ZnO NFs before and after GQD loading[44]. However, the peaks are significantly higher after GQD incorporation, indicating an enhanced UV absorption effect. Fig. 1(i) displays the infrared spectrum of ZnO NFs with and without GQDs. Peaks around 700 cm⁻¹ correspond to the flexible substrate, while peaks at 1019 cm⁻¹ , 1246 cm⁻¹ , and 1723 cm⁻¹ are attributed to C-O, C-H, and CO groups, respectively. These peaks indicate the presence of oxygen-containing functional groups in the GQDs, which result from the oxidation step and facilitate their solubility in water. Finally, Fig. 1(j) shows the XRD patterns of ZnO NFs with peaks at 31.73°, 34.34°, 36.29°, 47.41°, 56.45°, 62.82°, 66.50°, 67.76°, and 68.97°, corresponding to ZnO planes (100), (002), (101), (102), (110), (103), (200), (112), and (201). These patterns align with the standard spectrum (No. 00–036–1451) [45] and confirm the hexagonal fibrillar zincite crystal structure of the ZnO NFs.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used to examine the oxidation state and chemical composition of ZnO NFs. Fig. 2(a) displays the full XPS spectra of ZnO NFs and GQDs: ZnO NFs, revealing the presence of Zn (Zn 2p), O (O 1 s), and C (C 1 s) components. The Zn 2p spectra in Fig. 2(b) show two peaks at 1043.90 eV and 1020.82 eV, corresponding to Zn 2p₁/₂ and Zn 2p₃/₂, respectively, indicating a + 2 oxidation state. The energy difference between these peaks is 23 eV, consistent with literature values [24]. Compared to pristine ZnO NFs, the peaks for Zn 2p₁/₂ and Zn 2p₃/₂ in GQDs: ZnO NFs show a 0.3 eV shift to higher binding energies, suggesting an interaction between ZnO and GQDs. Fig. 2(c) shows the O 1 s spectra for ZnO NFs and GQDs: ZnO NFs, with peaks observed at 530.69 eV. These peaks are attributed to the O²⁻ ions within the ZnO lattice, and the differences between them are likely due to the interaction between GQDs and ZnO [46]. Similarly, the C 1 s spectra in Fig. 2(d) reveal significant peaks,

with the main peak at 283.88 eV indicating C-C bonds in the sp² configuration. Additional peaks at 287.69 eV and 285.37 eV correspond to carbon-oxygen double and single bonds, respectively, confirming the presence of carbon bonds in the GQDs: ZnO NFs [47,48]. Fig. 2(e) illustrates the interactions between GQDs: ZnO/PEDOS, where the oxygen-containing groups at the edges of GQDs can fill the oxygen vacancies on the ZnO surface while forming hydrogen bonds with the O atoms in the PEDOS structure. Additionally, the π-bonds of GQDs stack with the π-bonds in the PEDOS structure, creating π-π stacking. Consequently, GQDs bridge the electronic transport between ZnO and PEDOS, leading to a UV PD with enhanced photoresponse and shorter response times.

In order to study the optoelectronic performance of the flexible device, the working area as well as the light-exposed area of the flexible device was defined as shown in Fig. S4, and the light-exposed area of the flexible device was 0.24 cm2. The current-voltage characteristics of the device under different light intensities with 365 nm wavelength light were investigated, as shown in Fig. 3. Fig. 3(a) presents the I-T curve for the pure ZnO/ZnO device. Although this device shows a relatively typical response waveform, the photocurrent only reaches approximately 7 nA within 10 seconds of UV irradiation and does not achieve its maximum equilibrium value. Furthermore, as illustrated in Fig. 3(b), the single ZnO device exhibits both an extended response time and a low photocurrent. In contrast, Fig. 3(c) illustrates the I-T curve for the PET/ITO/ZnO/PEDOS/ITO/PET device, which displays a notably typical and well-defined response waveform. This device shows a stabilized photocurrent of about 50 nA within 10 seconds of UV irradiation. Due to the pyroelectric effect of the ZnO material [49], an abrupt output current is observed in the initial stages of UV irradiation, as shown in Fig. 3(d) at stage I. This immediate current results from the combined influence of photoelectric and pyroelectric effects, referred to as Ipyro+photo. Stage II in Fig. 3(d) demonstrates the stabilization of temperature changes on the ZnO surface following continued UV irradiation. During this phase, photogenerated electron pairs are converted into both photoelectric and thermal effects, resulting in a stabilized output current denoted as Iphoto+thermo. After the UV irradiation stops, the device's response rapidly declines to the dark current within a short period, eventually stabilizing, as depicted in stages III and IV of Fig. 3(d). The inclusion of PEDOS significantly reduces the response time of the device compared to pure ZnO UV photodetectors, with rise and fall times of 0.166/0.168 seconds. This improvement is due to PEDOS acting as an excellent hole-transporting layer, which facilitates rapid hole movement and forms a p-n heterojunction with ZnO, enhancing the separation of electron-hole pairs generated by the photoelectric effect. This greatly shortens the device's response time. The I-T curves for the PET/ITO/GQDs: ZnO/PEDOS/ITO/PET device, shown in Fig. 3(e), reveal that the device continues to exhibit typical waveform behavior even with the addition of GQDs. However, the presence of GQDs results in a smoother waveform, with the smoothed current reaching approximately 115 nA after 10 seconds of UV irradiation. Additionally, due to the thermal effect of GQDs under UV irradiation, the device demonstrates a stronger pyroelectric effect compared to ZnO/PEDOS alone. As shown in Fig. 3(f), the sharpness of Ipyro+photo is enhanced, resulting in a smoother output current. The photogenerated electron-hole pairs at the GQDs, aided by the formation of a p-n heterojunction between ZnO NFs and PEDOS, undergo rapid separation, leading to rise and fall times of 0.165/0.149 seconds, respectively. Finally, the device's response to varying light intensities was evaluated, with results displayed in Fig. 3(g). The smooth current of the device increases gradually with rising light intensity, accompanied by a corresponding increase in its maximum output current. This is attributed to the enhanced visibility of the pyroelectric effects of both ZnO and GQDs at higher light intensities. Additionally, the reverse pyroelectric effect occurs when UV light disappears. The I-t curves of the ZnO/GQDs: ZnO devices are shown in Fig. S5. Fig. S5(a) shows the I-t curve of ZnO/GQDs: ZnO under UV irradiation at 365 nm, 0.32 mW/cm2. It can be seen that the photocurrent of the device is enhanced by the introduction of GQDs, but the response time is still very long, and the photocurrent does not reach the maximum value at an irradiation period of 10 s. The photocurrent of the ZnO device is also enhanced by the introduction of GQDs. Fig. S5(b) shows the I-t curves of ZnO/GQDs: ZnO at 365 nm, 0.32 mW/cm2 UV irradiation, and 0.1 V bias voltage, in comparison, the photocurrent is greatly improved, but the response time is still very long. It can be seen that constructing p-n heterojunction is the most effective means to reduce the response time of the device. Fig. S6 shows the I-V curve of the ZnO/ZnO, GQD: ZnO/ZnO (Fig. S6(a)), ZnO/PEDOS, and GQD: ZnO/PEDOS (Fig. S6(c)) devices, and in order to see the current characteristics of the devices more clearly at 0 V bias, the curves are enlarged in the range from −0.05 V to 0.05 V, as shown in Fig. S6(b, d). It can be clearly seen that the current values of the two devices without PEDOS are almost 0 at 0 V bias, indicating that the ZnO/ZnO and GQD: ZnO/ZnO devices do not form a p-n heterojunction and do not have self-powered characteristics. In contrast, the current values of the ZnO/PEDOS and GQD: ZnO/PEDOS devices at 0 V bias are 0.17 and 0.25 μA, respectively, demonstrating that the ZnO and PEDOS form a p-n heterojunction, which gives the devices self-powered characteristics. Where the introduction of GQDs enhances the current values of the devices.

The performance of the device under varying light intensities is illustrated in Fig. S7. Fig. S7(a) shows how both the maximum output current and the maximum smooth current of the GQDs: ZnO/PEDOS device fluctuate with different light intensities. It is evident that as the light intensity increases, the photocurrent also increases. This increase in Ipyro+photo. Ipyro+photo is more pronounced due to the enhanced pyroelectric effects of ZnO and GQDs at higher light intensities. Fig. S7(b) depicts the relationship between responsivity and light intensity. According to Eq. (1), responsivity is inversely proportional to light intensity, which is generally reflected in the curve shown in Fig. S7. However, the maximum responsivity of 1.91 mA is observed at 0.56 mW/cm². This anomaly can be attributed to the improved contact of the device during this specific test, which results in a reduced dark current and consequently an increased responsivity. demonstrates the correlation between detection rate and light intensity. According to Eq. (2), D∗ is proportional to responsivity (R), and the data in Fig. S4 confirm this relationship. Fig. S7(d) presents the link between response time and light intensity. The rise time is defined as the duration required for the current to increase from 10 % to 90 % of the maximum photocurrent, while the fall time is the time needed for the current to decrease from 90 % to 10 % of the maximum photocurrent. The figure shows a general decrease in response time with increasing light intensity, except for a minor increase at 2.40 mW/cm². This indicates that, within a certain range, increasing light intensity helps reduce the device's response time. However, at excessively high light intensities, the increased number of photogenerated carriers can reduce the efficiency of carrier separation in the p-n heterojunction, thereby increasing the response time Fig. S8 displays the dark current of the device. It shows that the dark current is significantly lower at 0.56 mW/cm² compared to other light intensities, resulting in a larger responsivity (R) at this intensity.

To evaluate the performance of the flexible UV detector under bending conditions, the ties of disposable medical masks were attached to the upper and lower ends of the UV detector, as shown in the bending schematic illustrated in Fig. 4. Fig. 4(a), (d), and (g) display physical views of the flexible device at different bending angles, and tests were conducted under these conditions. Fig. 4(b) shows the I-T curves of the UV detector under 365 nm, 0.32 mW/cm² UV irradiation at a 60° bending angle, while Fig. 4(e) and (h) present the I-T curves for the same light intensity at 90° and 120° bending angles, respectively. From the comparison of these graphs, it is evident that the photocurrent (Ipyro+photo) decreases with increasing bending angle: at 60° bending, Ipyro+photo is 162.35 nA; at 90°, it is 109.53 nA; and at 120°, it is 83.25 nA. Despite this decrease, the device still exhibits a typical and repeatable waveform at each bending angle, indicating that the UV detector maintains its detection performance even at a 120° bending angle and demonstrates good flexibility and stability. The bending performance of the device without GQDs is shown in Fig. S9. At a 60° bending angle, the device maintains a normal response waveform, but the photocurrent is relatively low at around 30 nA. At bending angles of 90° and 120°, the I-T curve becomes irregular, and the photocurrent drops to approximately 0.5 nA. This is due to the rigidity of the ZnO NFs film, which tends to crack at larger bending angles. As shown in Fig. S10, significant cracking appeared on the ZnO nanofibers (NFs) layer after the bending performance test (see panels (a) and (b)). In Fig. S10(c), cracks measuring up to 197 nm are visible, supporting this observation. These cracks obstruct electron transport, leading to a decrease in photocurrent and device instability. In contrast, in the GQDs: ZnO/PEDOS device, GQDs are incorporated into the ZnO NFs layer, with many small spheres uniformly dispersed throughout. This dispersion reduces the formation of cracks and helps to bridge existing cracks, improving electron transmission. As a result, the device performs better under bending conditions Additionally, ZnO's high piezoelectric constant allows the device to regulate the energy band structure at the contact junction interface through piezoelectric polarization charges generated by external strain. This regulation helps control carrier generation, separation, transport, and recombination, mitigating the impact of cracking in the ZnO film during bending. The performance parameters of the flexible device at different bending angles were calculated according to formulas (1) and (2), as shown in Fig. S11. The testing conditions were 0.32 mW/cm2, 0 V bias, and room temperature. Fig. S11(a) shows the photocurrent of the flexible device under different bending conditions, where the photocurrent decreases with increasing bending angle. Similarly, the responsivity (Fig. S11(b)) and detectivity (Fig. S11(c)) follow the same trend. The response time (Fig. S11(d)) increases with the bending angle, leading to a decrease in the device's response speed. Under a 60° bending condition, the responsivity is 2.23 mA/W, the detectivity is 1.949 × 1010 Jones, and the response times are 0.074 s and 0.082 s, indicating better performance compared to the unbent state.

To further examine the device's performance under bending conditions, we compared the I-T curve at various bending angles with the I-T curve of the device in a flat, unbent state under the same illumination, as shown in Fig. 5(a). The device exhibits an increase in Ipyro+photo during 60° bending. At small bending angles, the two layers are in closer contact, and the PEDOS layer significantly enhances the performance of the ZnO layer. This improvement is attributed to the device's assembly method. The UV photodetector (PD) uses a flexible substrate, and during the face-to-face lamination process, some gaps inevitably form. Wrapping the device with PVA helps prevent these gaps from widening during use and reduces carrier spillover.

In addition, due to the piezoelectric photoelectric effect of ZnO, the device experiences a certain pressure when bent, which generates piezoelectric polarization charges that increase the number of carriers. These two effects make the flexible device more suitable for operation under small-angle bending. At small bending angles, the two layers in face-to-face lamination adhere more closely to each other due to external forces, reducing the gap between them. However, as the bending angle increases, the area of the device exposed to light decreases, leading to a reduction in the carriers generated by the photoelectric effect, pyroelectric effect, and piezoelectric photoelectric effect. Additionally, support from the GQDs diminishes over time, and the ZnO and PEDOS films on the flexible substrate gradually develop cracks, which impede carrier transmission and reduce the efficiency of carrier transport. This results in a decrease in the combined pyroelectric and photoelectric current (Ipyro+photo) of the UV photodetector (PD) as the bending angle increases. Therefore, this UV PD is more suitable for operation under small bending angles, making it more adaptable to complex working conditions and enhancing its practical significance. Furthermore, the I-T curves of this flexible device were tested after various numbers of bending cycles. As depicted in Fig. 5(b), the device was bent forward and backward at 90° and its I-T curves were measured in the straightened state after 100, 500, 700, and 1000 bending cycles, all under a test condition of 0.32 mW/cm². The results indicate that the device’s performance remains relatively stable with only minor fluctuations after bending cycles. However, after 1000 bending cycles, the structures of ZnO and GQDs were damaged, leading to a decrease in temperature sensitivity. At the same time, large cracks appear in the ZnO layer after many bending cycles, affecting the transmission of photogenerated carriers. With the combined effect of the two reasons, the device loses the pyroelectric effect after 1000 bends. Overall, the GQDs: ZnO/PEDOS device retains good UV detection activity even when bent and after multiple bending cycles.

The performance of the devices developed in this research is compared to various flexible UV photodetectors in Table 1. Notably, these devices offer distinct advantages. Under the conditions of 365 nm wavelength, 0.32 mW/cm2 intensity, 0 V bias voltage, and room temperature, the photodetectors achieved a maximum responsivity of 1.86 mA/W, a maximum detection rate of 1.96 × 1010 Jones, and optimal rise/fall times of 0.082/0.084 s. Remarkably, even when bent up to 120°, the photodetectors maintained their detection capabilities, with a maximum responsivity of 2.28 mA/W, a maximum detection rate of 1.95 × 1010 Jones, and optimal rise/fall times of 0.074/0.082 s. Furthermore, the devices demonstrated excellent mechanical durability, retaining their detection activity after up to 1000 bends. Unlike many flexible counterparts that rely on applied voltage for optimal performance, these devices exhibit remarkable performance even at zero bias voltage. Zhang et al. [41] designed a flexible UV photodetector based on silver nanowires, Ag NW@ZnO NR. Under a 5 V bias and 365 nm (1.3 mW/cm2) UV light exposure, it exhibits excellent optical response with a responsivity of 6.5 μA/W and response times of 2.6 s and 2.3 s. Additionally, it demonstrates good flexibility and outstanding mechanical stability after 1000 bending cycles.

Wu et al. [52] used borate polymers (BPs) as a co-substrate for the photoactive layer substrate and conductive ink to construct an integrated flexible UV photodetector. CuPc was chosen as the photoactive component, and a high response rate of 32.7 μA/W was achieved at 365 nm illumination. The FPD device exhibited stable photoresponse during 2000 UV stimulations with continuous 50 % stretching. In contrast, our PET/ITO/GQDs: ZnO/ITO/PET configuration demonstrates superior practicality due to enhanced overall flexibility. The inclusion of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) in the device structure not only improves flexibility but also offers advantages in response speed. This flexible device achieves self-powered detection with a response speed comparable to that of rigid devices [53,54] while ensuring high responsiveness. Further discussion on the flexibility aspect will be provided in the manuscript.

Fig. 6 illustrates the operation mechanism of PET/ITO/GQDs: ZnO/PEDOS/ITO/PET devices. Fig. 6(a) and (b) show the calculated values of the HOMO and LUMO levels of PEDOS, which are −5.06 eV and −1.23 eV, respectively. The LUMO of PEDOS is significantly shifted by 3.27 eV from the conduction band (CB) of ZnO (-4.5 eV), providing sufficient driving force for the effective separation of photogenerated carriers and thereby enhancing the utilization of these carriers. The operation of the device under dark conditions is depicted in Fig. 6(c). In the absence of light, the photoelectronic effect of ZnO is minimal, resulting in only a slight dark current that originates from the intrinsic nature of the material itself. However, when exposed to UV radiation at wavelengths equal to or greater than the band gap of ZnO, the photoelectron effect (Fig. 6(d)) combines with the pyroelectric effect (Fig. 6(e)), leading to the formation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. This occurs as electrons leap from the valence band (VB) to the conduction band (CB) of ZnO. Simultaneously, these photogenerated electron-hole pairs rapidly separate under the influence of the built-in electric field formed by the p-n heterojunction of ZnO and PEDOS. The electrons migrate toward the ZnO side, while the holes migrate toward the PEDOS side and eventually accumulate on both sides of the ITO/PET interface. This process enables the ZnO-based UV detector to respond quickly. Moreover, the addition of GQDs on the ZnO side allows both materials to generate photoelectron pairs under UV irradiation, which further enhances the photocurrent through efficient separation under the built-in electric field. The operation mechanism of the device under bending conditions is shown in Fig. 6(f). When the device is bent during use, stress is applied to the ZnO layer. This stress amplifies the photocurrent through the piezoelectric photoelectron effect, thereby improving the overall performance of the ZnO UV detector.

Fig. 7(a) and (b) show the surface binding energies of GQDs-ZnO NFs and PEDOS-GQDs: ZnO. The binding energy between ZnO NFs and GQDs is −8.19 eV, indicating a strong interaction and close contact between these components, which facilitates electron transfer between them. In contrast, the binding energy between PEDOS and GQDs: ZnO NFs is −1.03 eV, suggesting the formation of a heterojunction structure that generates an internal electric field, promoting the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. This mechanism endows the device with self-powered characteristics and enhances the performance of the UV photodetector.

However, the binding energy between PEDOS and GQDs: ZnO NFs is lower than that between GQDs and ZnO NFs, indicating reduced electron transfer capability between PEDOS and GQDs: ZnO NFs. Thus, when the device is bent at a small angle, electron transfer between them becomes more efficient. This corresponds with the performance tests, where the flexible device shows better performance when bent at 60° compared to its unbent state. Fig. 7(c) and (d) display the work functions of ZnO NFs and GQDs, where the work function is defined as the minimum energy required for an electron to move from the solid interior to its surface. The work function of ZnO NFs is 8.43 eV, while that of GQDs is 5.99 eV. The lower work function of GQDs facilitates the generation of photogenerated electrons. Consequently, the addition of GQDs increases the number of photogenerated carriers in the device, enhancing the UV detection performance of the UV photodetector. This theoretically demonstrates that the introduction of GQDs can improve ZnO's response to UV light.

In summary, we have developed a flexible UV photodetector with a PET/ITO/GQDs: ZnO NFs/PEDOS/ITO/PET structure, which demonstrates excellent flexibility and stability, along with outstanding responsiveness and low response time. Due to the combined effects of photoelectric, thermoelectric, and piezoelectric effects of ZnO, the device exhibits remarkable UV photodetection capabilities. The p-n heterojunction formed between PEDOS and ZnO, as well as the Se atoms in the PEDOS structure and the oxygen-rich functional groups at the edges of the GQDs interact with the oxygen vacancies on the ZnO NFs surface, enhance the photoelectric current and shorten the response time of the device. The close contact between the ZnO plate-like "petals" helps maintain the integrity of the ZnO layer and alleviate cracking issues, at the same time, GQDs provide structural support to the ZnO layer, helping to maintain relative structural integrity during bending. Under 365 nm UV light, 0 V bias voltage, and room temperature conditions, the device achieves a maximum responsivity of 1.86 mA/W, a maximum detectivity of 1.96 × 1010 Jones, and optimal rise/fall times of 0.082/0.084 s. During bending, the maximum responsivity increases to 2.28 mA/W, the maximum detectivity is 1.95 × 1010 Jones, and the rise/fall times are shortened to 0.074/0.082 s. At a 120° bending angle, the device has a responsivity of 0.91 mA/W and a detectivity of 7.339 × 109 Jones. Even after 1000 bends at a 90° angle, the device retains UV photodetection capability with a responsivity of 1.02 mA/W and a detectivity of 9.930 × 109 Jones. This work provides a viable method for developing high-performance and fast-response ZnO-based flexible UV photodetectors.
